title: Web Data, Fetch, Storage, and Security Boundaries · Scenario Checkpoint
summary: Load and cache public service data while handling hostile input, offline states, privacy, and response failures. Every item uses a fresh case and misconception-specific distractors. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for a resilient privacy-aware service-data client. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Use fetch with explicit method, headers, body, status checks, parsing, abort, and failure-state behavior.
Construct and parse URL, URLSearchParams, FormData, and encoded payloads without manual delimiter bugs.
Parse, validate, transform, and serialize JSON while accounting for unsupported values and hostile object shape.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.javascript:
// javascript-basics cumulative stakeholder program.
// Add one runnable behavior, assertion, changed case, and evidence return per activity.

steps.title: Reconstruct the defensible process
steps.instruction: Order the Fetch Response evidence cycle for this quiz task before implementing it.
steps.why: Correct order keeps Fetch Response assumptions visible before execution and verifies its result against a boundary case afterward.

content.text: Fetch unknown status JSON containing a hostile "" label, cancel request 1, render with textContent, minimize stored data, and expose a recoverable error without leaking credentials.

content.title: Ordering constraint
content.text: Order work so Fetch Response has a declared contract before execution and a boundary or counterexample after it.

hints: Name the Fetch Response precondition or invariant that the quiz case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: Fetch unknown status JSON containing a hostile "" label, cancel request 1, render with textContent, minimize stored data, and expose a recoverable error without leaking credentials.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: Fetch rejects its promise for every HTTP error status.

options.text: Declare inputs, observable output, ownership, and the runtime behavior being tested
Trace values, bindings, receivers, mutations, and queued work before editing code
Implement the smallest behavior and retain an assertion over the actual result
Run boundary and changed-event cases, inspect failures, and remove accidental coupling

steps.title: Defend one difficult decision
steps.instruction: Write the quiz evidence note for Origin Permissions: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — Fetch unknown status JSON containing a hostile "" label, cancel request 4, render with textContent, minimize stored data, and expose a recoverable error without leaking credentials.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Explain origin, CORS, CSP, credentials, workers, and permission prompts as layered browser boundaries.
